title: The Blackout IS the Story: How the US Controls the Evidentiary Record of the Iran War
subtitle: The US has not merely fought a war — it is fighting an information war about what happened.
author: The Siphoned Truth
content:
The United States has not merely fought a war in Iran — it is fighting an information war about what happened. The indefinite blackout on commercial satellite imagery covering the conflict zone is not operational security. It is evidentiary suppression: a coordinated move to ensure that when history is written about this war, only one side's version reaches the record.

On April 5, Planet Labs announced it would "indefinitely withhold visuals of Iran and the conflict region" at the explicit request of the U.S. government. The restriction is retroactive to March 9, 2026, replacing a previous 14-day delay with what the company calls a "managed distribution" model. Vantor, formerly Maxar, independently implemented similar controls covering areas where U.S. forces are "actively operating." No timeline has been given for restoration.

This is the first time in modern history the U.S. government has imposed a third-party imagery restriction on commercial satellite providers covering a U.S. military operation. Previous restrictions — on imagery of North Korean facilities, for example — were narrow and targeted. This one is broad, indefinite, and applied to the entire conflict zone across multiple providers. The pattern itself is the statement.

What they're not letting you see

The restriction is not applied uniformly across all targets. It is concentrated on the areas where the U.S. and Israel have made the most specific claims about Iranian military losses. Centcom has published casualty figures and damage assessments. The Pentagon claims Iranian air defense systems, missile facilities, and command centers have been destroyed or degraded. These are the precise claims that independent researchers, journalists, and allied governments cannot verify without commercial satellite imagery.

The Washington Post reported in May that satellite imagery — obtained before the blackout took full effect — showed Iranian strikes had damaged or destroyed at least 228 structures at U.S. military sites across the Middle East, far exceeding official U.S. acknowledgments. That report relied on imagery collected before the indefinite withhold was imposed. New reporting on the same site since March 9 has relied on a small number of images from European Sentinel satellites and a handful of Vantor images published under the "public interest" exception to the blackout policy.

Meanwhile, reconstruction continues at the very sites the U.S. claims to have destroyed. Satellite imagery analyzed by GlobalSecurity.org and published May 13 shows excavation equipment and cranes still positioned near the Fordow fuel enrichment plant's northern shaft entrance — where the crater from the initial strike was clearly visible, but where underground centrifuge halls appear structurally intact. New roofing has been erected over damaged buildings at Natanz. At Parchin, a concrete structure has been built over a new facility and buried under soil — a hardening posture consistent with ongoing military use, not the demolished site the U.S. has described. Iranian state media has referenced what it calls "evidence gaps" in U.S. claims, a narrative already being amplified by Chinese and Russian state outlets.

The financial architecture of compliance

The satellite companies did not resist. Planet Labs derives approximately 60% of its revenue from defense and intelligence contracts — up from roughly 20% in 2022. Vantor won $205 million in contracts with Middle East and North African nations in July 2025. These are not neutral commercial entities being leaned on by government pressure. They are financially dependent on the same defense ecosystem that requested the blackout.

As Jake Godin, a senior researcher at Bellingcat, told NPR: "The big problem is that it becomes very hard to fact check things like videos" without satellite imagery. Jeffrey Lewis of Middlebury College put it more bluntly: "States tend to be extremely jittery about how wars are reported when they themselves have some national investment in the outcome."

The gap that tells the story

Non-U.S. commercial providers still operate in the region, though with their own constraints. ICEYE, the Finnish SAR satellite operator, and Airbus continue to sell imagery. European Sentinel satellites — funded by EU governments, not subject to U.S. commercial restrictions — provide lower-resolution optical and SAR data that researchers have used to detect damage at multiple Iranian military sites. Bellingcat released a damage proxy tool in April that uses Sentinel-1 SAR data to identify probable destruction by comparing post-conflict imagery against a one-year historical baseline.

But the U.S. restriction specifically covers the high-resolution imagery that would allow detailed verification of specific claims: whether a specific building was destroyed, whether a tunnel entrance collapsed, whether a facility is operational. The gap between what Sentinel-2's 10-meter resolution can show and what Planet's sub-meter imagery could confirm is the gap the U.S. government has purchased — at least for now.

The pattern from the North Korea case is instructive: when the U.S. restricts information about something, the restriction itself leaks what it's protecting. Here, the blackout covers the precise geographic area where U.S. claims about Iranian losses are most specific. The companies comply voluntarily. The claims cannot be independently verified. Reconstruction is visible only in what little non-U.S. imagery remains available.

The U.S. is not fighting the Iran war blind. It has satellite coverage. It has the intelligence. What it is doing is ensuring that when the historical record is assembled — by journalists, by researchers, by allied governments, by future historians — the primary visual evidence will be what the U.S. government chooses to release, when it chooses to release it.

The restriction is not about protecting operational security. It is about controlling the moment at which the evidentiary record of the war is written. The blackout is not a side effect of the war. It is part of the war.

Word count: approximately 700
